title: Natural Disaster Resilience and Recovery Accountability Act
short_title: Natural Disaster Resilience and Recovery Accountability Act
plain_summary: This law creates a special group of 15 people to study how the government helps when natural disasters happen. The group will look at what the government does to get ready for disasters like hurricanes and floods, and how it helps people recover afterward. They will figure out what works well and what needs to be fixed so the government can do a better job and not waste money. The group has two years to write a report with their ideas and give it to Congress.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Natural Disaster Resilience and Recovery Accountability Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Commission on Federal Natural Disaster Resilience and Recovery
sections.text:
This section creates a special group called the Commission on Federal Natural Disaster Resilience and Recovery. The group will be part of the Office of Management and Budget (a government office that handles the federal budget).

The Commission means this special group. The Director means the head of the Office of Management and Budget. A natural disaster means weather events like floods, earthquakes, tornadoes, hurricanes, ice storms, and very hot or cold weather. It can also include other events the Commission decides are appropriate. The Office means the Office of Management and Budget.

The Commission's job is to study and suggest changes to make federal disaster programs work better and cost less money. These programs help communities prepare for disasters and recover after disasters happen.

The Commission will have fifteen members chosen by the Director of the Office of Management and Budget. One member must work for the Office of Management and Budget. The other fourteen members should come from different places and backgrounds when possible. They should include officials from state, local, and tribal governments, private companies, and nonprofit organizations. They should represent both city and rural areas.

The members can include people who work in emergency management, firefighters, police officers, emergency medical workers, health care providers, public health experts, government officials with disaster experience, elected leaders, infrastructure and cybersecurity experts, and people who represent individuals with disabilities and other special needs.

When choosing members, the Director should pick people who know about disaster preparation and recovery, emergency management at different government levels, important infrastructure, transportation, housing, and how different levels of government work together.

All members must be chosen within one hundred twenty days after this law is signed. The Commission will elect a chairperson by getting at least eight votes from members. The vice chairperson will be the member who works for the Office of Management and Budget.

Members who do not work for the federal government will not be considered federal employees except for injury compensation and legal claims. They can get paid for travel expenses like federal workers do. Members who already work for the federal government will not get extra pay for serving on the Commission.

The Commission must hold its first meeting within two hundred forty days after this law is signed. They can meet in person or online to hold hearings, gather information, and hear from witnesses.

Many federal agencies must help and advise the Commission. These include the Small Business Administration, Department of Agriculture, Department of Homeland Security, Department of Labor, Department of the Interior, Department of Energy, Department of Transportation, Department of Housing and Urban Development, Environmental Protection Agency, Department of Justice, Federal Emergency Management Agency, Army Corps of Engineers, Corporation for National and Community Service, Department of Commerce, Department of the Treasury, Office of Management and Budget, and any other agencies the Commission needs.

The Commission can ask any federal agency for information it needs to do its work. When the Commission chairperson asks, the agency head must provide that information. The Commission can use regular mail services like other government agencies.

The chairperson and vice chairperson must create written rules for how the Commission operates, with at least eight members voting to approve these rules. The rules must include how many members need to be present to conduct official business.

The Commission chairperson can hire and fire support staff without following normal government hiring rules. The Commission must choose an executive director with at least eight members voting to approve. The chairperson can set pay for the executive director and staff up to a certain government pay level. Federal employees can be assigned to help the Commission without losing their regular job benefits.

Within two years of its first meeting, the Commission must give a final report to two congressional committees. The report must include specific suggestions to improve how the federal government handles disaster preparation and recovery. It must review all federal disaster programs and activities, including their budgets and goals. It must list all federal disaster programs with their funding, goals, legal authority, and related grant programs. It should consider changing how different federal agencies share disaster responsibilities. It should suggest policies to make federal disaster programs work better, reach more people, reduce waste, and avoid duplication. It can also address other disaster topics the Commission thinks are important.

The report should include both short-term and long-term suggestions for laws and administrative changes. Starting one hundred eighty days after this law is signed, and every one hundred eighty days after that, the Commission must give progress reports to the same congressional committees.

The Commission will end sixty days after submitting its final report. The money to run this Commission will come from funds already given to the Office of Management and Budget. No new money is approved for this law.
